pattern: Only the absolute per-index gaps matter, so pool both budgets and greedily shave the largest gaps first, which is done efficiently by lowering all gaps to a common ceiling and distributing the remainder.
hints: Modifying either array changes one term by adjusting its absolute difference, so reduce the problem to a multiset of absolute gaps and a single combined budget.
Spending one unit on the currently largest gap gives the biggest drop in the squared sum, so the optimal target is to flatten the top gaps to some level.
Binary search or sort to find the level the budget can flatten everything to, hand out any leftover units to the still-highest gaps, then sum the squares.

edge_inputs.description: no budget so the raw squared difference stands
budget large enough to zero every gap
already equal arrays leave nothing to do
budget partially flattens, leftover hits a subset

stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 300))
    nums1 = [rng.randint(0, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(0, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    total = sum(abs(a - b) for a, b in zip(nums1, nums2))
    k1 = rng.randint(0, total + 5)
    k2 = rng.randint(0, total + 5)
    return [nums1, nums2, k1, k2]
stress.note: budgets bounded near total gap so both saturated and partial regimes appear
